fact: August 18 is exactly 230 days into the year - that's like counting to 230 without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many spiders start spinning their biggest, most impressive webs because bug season is at its peak.
The sun rises about 2 minutes later than it did just a week ago - summer is secretly sneaking away!
Cicadas sound extra loud around August 18 because it hits during their peak screaming season when they're trying to find mates.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, this date falls in the middle of winter, so kids might be building snowmen while you're sweating.
The moon on August 18 can appear extra orange or red when it's low in the sky because summer air is thick with dust and humidity.
Sunflowers are typically at their most gigantic around this time - their heavy heads full of seeds make them bow like they're taking a bow on stage.
August 18 sits right in the heart of Leo season, which means the sun is in the constellation that looks like a lion.
In many places, this is when morning dew gets so heavy that spider webs look like they're covered in tiny diamonds.
August 18 has exactly 135 days left until the end of the year - you could count down on your fingers and toes 13 times plus 5 more!
Tree leaves often start their very first hints of color change around now in northern climates, even though you probably can't see it yet - it's like nature's secret preparation.
In the Arctic, August 18 means the sun is setting earlier each day by about 20 minutes compared to the week before - that's like losing a whole TV show of daylight every few days.
